chunk_summary: Case-based management decisions in SAM discharge and outpatient care emphasize tailored approaches based on clinical metrics, caregiver reliability, and follow-up adherence to ensure safe recovery and prevent complications.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of using admission criteria for discharge decisions in SAM management.
Learn to address feeding effectiveness in infants with low weight-for-length and weak suck.
Develop strategies for tracing and managing defaulters in outpatient CMAM programs.
Recognize and manage SAM with complications such as severe pneumonia and suspected HIV.
key_facts: Discharge should require repeated edema-free stability and not rely solely on weight-for-height normalization.
MUAC <125 mm indicates continued therapy despite WHZ improvement.
Infants with weak suck require urgent lactation support and feeding effectiveness assessment, not just therapeutic milk.
Defaulters in outpatient CMAM must be traced immediately to assess illness, social barriers, or danger signs.
Severe pneumonia in SAM with suspected HIV requires broad antibiotics, high-dose cotrimoxazole for PCP, and ART initiation after stabilization.
reference_quote: Correct approach is to anchor recovery to the admission criterion and require repeated edema-free stability; discordance is common and continued therapy with close follow-up is safer than single-metric discharge. (WHO Guideline, 2023)
document_key_topics: Follow-up and caregiver reliability in SAM treatment
